description: Enumerations for character classification, psychology, motivation, and structural role.

values.definition: The protagonist who faces the central ordeal and drives the main story action.
values.example_usage: The primary protagonist.

values.definition: A wise guide who advises, trains, or equips the hero.
values.example_usage: A father figure, teacher, or experienced advisor character.

values.definition: A figure who tests the hero at boundaries between story worlds.
values.example_usage: A character who blocks entry or challenges the hero's readiness.

values.definition: A character or force that announces change or adventure, catalyzing the call to action.
values.example_usage: The messenger who delivers the inciting news.

values.definition: A character with ambiguous, shifting loyalty who disrupts the hero's certainty.
values.example_usage: A love interest or ally whose true allegiance is unclear.

values.definition: The dark reflection or antagonist who embodies the hero's fears or rejected qualities.
values.example_usage: The primary villain or dark double of the protagonist.

values.definition: A companion who supports the hero's journey without a competing agenda.
values.example_usage: A loyal friend or sidekick.

values.definition: A comic relief or chaos agent who subverts order and forces adaptation.
values.example_usage: A character who breaks rules and creates unpredictable situations.

values.definition: A relatable, ordinary character who grounds the story in recognizable experience.
values.example_usage: A working-class character who represents the reader's perspective.

values.definition: A character primarily driven by passion, connection, and the pursuit of relationship.
values.example_usage: The romantic lead whose primary motivation is love.

values.definition: A protector or nurturer defined by selfless service to others.
values.example_usage: A parent, nurse, or community leader figure.

values.definition: A truth-seeker and philosopher who values knowledge above all else.
values.example_usage: A scholar, detective, or intellectual mentor character.

values.definition: An optimistic character who seeks safety and believes the world is good.
values.example_usage: A naive protagonist at story start; a child character.

values.definition: A character who overthrows what isn't working and challenges established order.
values.example_usage: A revolutionary, outlaw, or rule-breaker protagonist.

values.definition: A character driven by control, responsibility, and the maintenance of order.
values.example_usage: A king, CEO, patriarch, or institutional authority figure.

values.definition: A character rejected by society who operates from the margins.
values.example_usage: An exile, pariah, or social outsider.

values.definition: An innovator or artist driven to bring something new into existence.
values.example_usage: An inventor, writer, or visionary character.

values.definition: A seeker of new experiences who cannot stay confined by convention.
values.example_usage: An adventurer, traveler, or character driven by curiosity.

values.definition: A character who transforms reality — themselves or their world.
values.example_usage: A literal or metaphorical transformer; a catalyst character.

values.definition: A character who lives in the moment and uses humor to reveal truth.
values.example_usage: A comic figure who speaks difficult truths through laughter.

values.definition: A character who breaks rules deliberately and rejects social contracts.
values.example_usage: A criminal protagonist or anti-hero defined by transgression.

wound.description: Core psychological wound categories (23 variants: 13 umbrella + 10 fine-grained).

values.definition: The character was left, rejected, or not chosen by someone whose love they needed.
Someone the character trusted acted against them or violated their confidence.
The character blames themselves for a harm, loss, or failure — real or perceived.
The character experienced physical, emotional, or sexual violence.
The character internalized the belief that they are fundamentally defective or unworthy.
The character carries unresolved mourning for a person, identity, or future lost.
An institution, system, or authority the character relied on failed or actively harmed them.
The character was unable to protect themselves or others from harm.
The character's authentic self was denied, suppressed, or punished by others.
The character experienced systematic unfairness or was punished for someone else's actions.
The character's needs — physical or emotional — were chronically ignored.
The character survived something that others did not and carries the weight of that survival.
The character lost their home, culture, or sense of belonging through exile or migration.
A parent left, died, or was emotionally absent at a critical developmental moment.
A romantic partner left, betrayed, or rejected the character at a defining moment.
The character was ostracized, shunned, or expelled from a community they valued.
A trusted institution (school, church, military, company) failed or actively harmed the character.
The character's failure, weakness, or secret was exposed publicly.
The character was taught that a core aspect of who they are (race, sexuality, gender, disability) is wrong.
The character lost the role, mission, or belief system that defined their identity.
The character made a decision or took an action with devastating, irreversible consequences.
The character acted against their own values — possibly under coercion — and cannot forgive themselves.
The character was controlled, over-protected, or had their autonomy systematically denied.

alignment.description: 9-cell moral alignment grid encoding law/chaos and good/evil axes.

values.definition: Acts according to a code of honor; believes rules and goodness are compatible.
Acts to do good without strong loyalty to rules or chaos.
Acts for good but resists rules and authority.
Follows rules and order without strong moral commitment to good or evil.
Avoids taking sides; balances all forces.
Acts on impulse; values personal freedom over rules or ethics.
Pursues self-interest or harmful goals within or through established rules and hierarchy.
Does whatever is necessary to achieve harmful goals without preferring order or chaos.
Acts with destructive intent, rejecting all rules and caring nothing for others.

role.description: Functional narrative role (what the character does to the plot). Distinct from archetype (psychological template).

values.definition: The story is organized around their want and arc.
The secondary significant character with their own arc.
Opposes the protagonist's want, creating central conflict.
Primary romantic other to the protagonist or deuteragonist.
Guides, trains, or advises other characters.
Receives the protagonist's internal thoughts; enables exposition of inner life.
Supports the protagonist without a competing central agenda.
Exists structurally to contrast the protagonist, making their qualities visible.
Triggers change in others without necessarily having their own arc.
Connects two social worlds or group of characters.
Absent character whose presence is felt through others' psychology.
Agent of chaos; disrupts status quo and tests other characters.
Tests the protagonist before allowing access to the next world or stage.
Announces change; delivers the call to adventure.
Shifts alliance or identity; creates doubt about motives.
Works against the protagonist from within the same group; impedes without being the antagonist.
Minor character with no arc; functional presence only.

drive_model.description: Character motivational framework — the primary 'spring' driving the character across the whole story.

values.definition: The character is driven primarily by a core psychological wound and its resulting misbelief (Weiland/Cron).
values.example_usage: A trauma survivor whose wound shapes every decision.

values.definition: The character is driven by what they want — a conscious goal or longing.
values.example_usage: A character who wants wealth, love, or recognition above all else.

values.definition: The character is driven by obligation — to a person, code, institution, or role.
values.example_usage: A soldier, parent, or caretaker who acts from responsibility.

values.definition: The character is driven by epistemology — the need to understand, know, or see clearly.
values.example_usage: A detective, scholar, or character whose arc is about seeing the truth.

values.definition: The character is driven by questions of meaning, purpose, and identity.
values.example_usage: A character in existential crisis; a character questioning the point of their life.

arc_type.description: Character arc trajectory across the whole story.

values.definition: Character overcomes the lie they believe and embraces the truth they need (Weiland).
values.example_usage: Classic hero journey arc; comedy arc.

values.definition: Character rejects the truth and embraces the lie — tragedy or corruption.
values.example_usage: Tragic protagonist; villain arc.

values.definition: Character already holds the truth and functions to catalyze change in others.
values.example_usage: Western hero; mentor figure; some action protagonists.

values.definition: Character changes but not along the main thematic axis of the story.
values.example_usage: Supporting character with a small personal arc.

values.definition: Character begins believing the truth, loses it, ends in tragedy.
values.example_usage: Idealist broken by experience.

values.definition: Character knowingly compromises their principles and becomes a worse version of themselves.
values.example_usage: Breaking Bad-style arc; slow moral decline.

values.definition: Character has no arc — they are the same at the end as at the start.
values.example_usage: Functional characters; true walk-ons; some ensemble members.

actant.description: Greimas actantial role — the character's structural position in the story's narrative grammar.

values.definition: The character who desires the object and drives the main action.
values.example_usage: The protagonist.

values.definition: The entity, value, or goal that the subject seeks.
values.example_usage: May be a person, quality, or abstract goal — not always a character.

values.definition: The motivating authority who initiates the quest or establishes the mission.
values.example_usage: A king who sends the hero; a moral imperative; societal pressure.

values.definition: The entity who benefits from the subject achieving the object.
values.example_usage: May be the subject themselves or another character.

values.definition: An entity who aids the subject in achieving the object.
values.example_usage: Allies, tools, lucky events.

values.definition: An entity who impedes the subject from achieving the object.
values.example_usage: Antagonist, obstacles, opposing forces.

conflict_style.description: Character's default interpersonal conflict approach — how they habitually respond when facing opposition.

values.definition: Withdraws from conflict entirely; sidesteps confrontation at any cost.
values.example_usage: A character who changes the subject, leaves the room, or goes quiet when tensions rise.

values.definition: Seeks a mutually acceptable solution; engages conflict as a problem to solve.
values.example_usage: A diplomat character who always looks for the middle ground.

values.definition: Seeks to win the conflict outright; prioritizes their position over the relationship.
values.example_usage: A character who raises their voice, pulls rank, or escalates until the other backs down.

values.definition: Disengages emotionally while remaining physically present; shuts down.
values.example_usage: A character who goes blank-faced and monosyllabic under pressure.

values.definition: Redirects the conflict through humor, subject changes, or reframing — never addresses it head-on.
values.example_usage: A character who cracks jokes when the argument gets serious, or pivots to someone else's problem.

attachment_pattern.description: Bowlby/Ainsworth attachment style governing behavior in close relationships, especially under stress.

values.definition: Comfortable with intimacy and autonomy; seeks closeness without anxiety or avoidance.
values.example_usage: A character who can ask for help, express needs, and tolerate distance.

values.definition: Hyperactivates attachment system under stress; seeks excessive reassurance and closeness.
values.example_usage: A character who becomes clingy, panics at perceived distance, or reads abandonment into neutral signals.

values.definition: Deactivates attachment system under stress; suppresses needs and maintains emotional distance.
values.example_usage: A character who pulls away when things get intimate, insists they're fine, or refuses to depend on anyone.

values.definition: Oscillates between anxious and avoidant strategies; approach-avoidance conflict in close bonds.
values.example_usage: A character who desperately wants closeness but sabotages it when it arrives — often linked to trauma.
